title: ATM Robbery
description: Rip an ATM fast, move it cleanly, and disappear before the response closes the route.
longDescription:
ATM robbery is a quick, loud money job that rewards speed more than comfort. The score is simple on paper, but the real pressure comes from timing the hit, securing transport, and getting the machine out of the area before the city catches up.

It works best for crews who like sharp windows and short-notice decisions. A clean run feels efficient and professional, while a sloppy one leaves a trail of panic, witnesses, and attention behind it.

title: Shop Robbery
description: Pressure late-night businesses, control the scene, and survive the short response window.
longDescription:
Shop robbery is one of the cleanest ways to generate direct public tension. The target is small enough to feel immediate, but the moment the pressure starts, clerks, bystanders, and police all become part of the story.

These jobs reward composure and roleplay just as much as they reward speed. A crew that can control the moment without panicking will always feel more dangerous than one that relies on noise alone.

title: Smash and Grab
description: Hit a storefront hard, grab value quickly, and turn noise into profit before the street locks down.
longDescription:
Smash and grab jobs are built around impact. They are not subtle, and that is exactly why they work so well for players who want fast movement, immediate risk, and the feeling that every second on scene is borrowed time.

The appeal is not just the payout. It is the chaos of a short violent window, the scramble to get clear, and the chance that one bold move turns into a pursuit, a witness problem, or a new rivalry.

title: Vangelico Robbery
description: Target high-end jewellery with a score that feels sharper, richer, and far more exposed than a corner-shop hit.
longDescription:
Vangelico robbery is where retail crime turns elegant and dangerous at the same time. The setting is premium, the target is valuable, and the whole job feels more deliberate than a rough smash at a low-tier business.

It is ideal for crews who want style as well as risk. When played well, it gives the underworld a luxury target that still carries the same threat of alarms, response time, and public fallout.

title: Heist Contracts
description: Take contract jobs that connect smaller crews to bigger scores and organised-crime progression.
longDescription:
Heist contracts are the bridge between small-time crime and serious operations. They give characters a reason to meet fixers, complete prep, prove reliability, and earn access to jobs that feel larger than anything you can do alone.

This is a strong lane for crews that want progression without rushing straight to the top. It creates structure, gatekeeping, and the sense that organised crime has levels instead of one flat menu of cash grabs.

title: Pacific Bank Heist
description: Pull one of the biggest public scores on the map with planning, coordination, and serious consequences.
longDescription:
Pacific Bank is the kind of hit that should feel like an event, not a routine job. The target is prestigious, the pressure is immediate, and the aftermath can affect police, crews, and the wider city long after the score ends.

It suits players who enjoy high-commitment scenes with roles, fallback plans, and the constant chance of collapse. Success should feel earned. Failure should leave a mark.

title: Train Heist
description: Board a moving score, manage timing, and turn raw logistics into a cinematic getaway.
longDescription:
Train heists feel different from static robberies because the target never stops moving. That one change creates a completely different type of tension around timing, positioning, escape, and how much pressure a crew can handle once the job is in motion.

This activity is perfect for players who want action-heavy crime without losing the need for prep. It rewards timing, nerve, and the willingness to commit when the moment arrives.

title: Yacht Heist
description: Infiltrate luxury at the waterfront and turn a quiet marina into a premium covert score.
longDescription:
Yacht heists bring a different texture to criminal RP. Instead of high-street panic or city-centre noise, the atmosphere is cleaner, quieter, and far more dependent on stealth, positioning, and a polished crew dynamic.

That contrast is what makes the job special. It gives players a luxury target that still feels dangerous, but in a way that leans on poise and discipline instead of raw chaos.

title: Chop Shop
description: Strip hot vehicles, break them down, and keep the illegal parts economy moving behind closed doors.
longDescription:
Chop shop work is one of the best ways to make vehicle crime feel like a proper underworld trade. The payoff is not just in the car itself, but in the hidden garage work, the parts pipeline, and the idea that stolen vehicles do not simply vanish on their own.

This route suits characters who like industrial, behind-the-scenes criminal RP. It is less public than a robbery, but no less important when crews need money, supplies, and somewhere dirty work can happen out of sight.

title: VIN Scratching
description: Alter vehicle identity, hide hot cars, and support the deeper fraud side of the vehicle scene.
longDescription:
VIN scratching turns vehicle crime into something more technical than simple theft. The job is about cleaning identities, reducing traceability, and making dangerous assets usable for longer than a single hot run.

It works especially well for players who enjoy quiet competence and illegal tradecraft. The role creates value for everyone else in the ecosystem without needing a public gunfight every time money changes hands.

title: Fake Plates
description: Build clean-looking covers for dirty vehicles and make future jobs harder to trace.
longDescription:
Fake plates are prep work, but good prep is what keeps bigger jobs alive. A vehicle that looks wrong or reads hot at the wrong moment can kill a plan before the crew even reaches the target.

That makes this activity more valuable than it looks at first glance. It feeds robberies, transport, and escape routes while giving technically minded criminals a lane that feels useful every single day.

title: Slash Tires
description: Sabotage parked vehicles to intimidate rivals, cause problems, or spark street-level retaliation.
longDescription:
Slash tires is a smaller act of crime, but it can create outsized roleplay when used well. It is quick, mean, and personal, which makes it ideal for messages, escalation, and low-cost disruption when a crew wants to apply pressure.

Because the act is simple, the story around it matters most. Used properly, it becomes a warning shot that can pull characters into feuds, revenge, and broader neighbourhood tension.

title: Drug System
description: Work the local product scene, manage repeat customers, and build criminal presence one handoff at a time.
longDescription:
Drug dealing gives the underworld its day-to-day rhythm. It is one of the clearest ways for characters to build street presence, create repeat business, and turn small transactions into larger territory problems over time.

The strength of this route is consistency. It keeps people active between major jobs and helps the criminal world feel lived in, competitive, and always one mistake away from unwanted heat.

title: Pickpocket
description: Lift cash and valuables from crowds with a low-profile route built on timing and nerve.
longDescription:
Pickpocketing is one of the purest opportunist crimes on the page. It depends on movement, awareness, and the confidence to work close to ordinary people without turning the whole scene loud.

That makes it perfect for characters who prefer subtle pressure over brute force. It is quick to understand, easy to weave into city life, and strong for players who enjoy stealthy personal risk instead of crew-scale violence.

title: Graffiti
description: Mark territory, send messages, and turn public walls into visible criminal presence.
longDescription:
Graffiti is not just vandalism when it is tied to reputation and territory. It gives crews a way to announce themselves, taunt rivals, and turn ordinary public space into part of the underworld's conversation.

This activity works especially well for players who like style, symbolism, and smaller criminal acts with longer social reach. The mark itself is quick. The message behind it can last much longer.

title: Grave Digging
description: Take a stranger, darker route into underworld money with secluded jobs and uneasy atmosphere.
longDescription:
Grave digging stands out because it feels different from the rest of the criminal menu. The locations are quieter, the mood is worse, and the work carries an unsettling edge that turns even a simple job into something memorable.

It suits players who want criminal RP that feels eerie rather than flashy. That difference gives the underworld more texture and makes the activity feel like a proper odd-job lane instead of recycled robbery content.

title: Illegal Tools Crafting
description: Produce the dirty kit other criminals need and become useful long before a robbery starts.
longDescription:
Illegal tools crafting turns preparation into a criminal identity of its own. Not everyone needs to be the person kicking the door or grabbing the cash when there is real value in being the one who can supply the gear.

That makes this route perfect for players who want influence through utility. It feeds bigger jobs, creates trade relationships, and gives the wider underworld a reason to know your character by name.

title: Fuel Siphoning
description: Steal fuel quietly from the city's working vehicles in a slower utility-crime route.
longDescription:
Fuel siphoning is designed as a quieter kind of theft built around patience, positioning, and supply rather than spectacle. It gives crews another way to feed the underworld through resources instead of direct cash extraction.

This route is marked as coming soon, but it already fits the page because it rounds out the utility side of the criminal economy. When it lands, it should feel like dirty logistics with real roleplay value, not just another copy of a robbery loop.
